items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
I’m having nagging doubts again…
Shpak had a tape recorder,
the envoy had a medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: Partly successful: the core catchphrase is preserved via “nagging doubts,” which keeps the mock-heroic self-dramatizing tone reasonably well. However, the second clause is semantically off: in context, it’s not possession (“had”) but a parallel of losses/objects involved (each one’s corresponding item is implicated/has gone missing). “Had” reads like simple past possession and weakens the comic detective-like linkage. Also, “envoy” is acceptable, but the reference’s “ambassador” better matches the official diplomatic role and the parallelism with Shpak’s status-item list.

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation:
So nice, Tsar!
So nice, hello, Tsar!

Tsar, so nice!
Hello, Tsar! So nice!
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: The translation preserves the key comic mechanism: repetitive, banal politeness (“so nice”) and obsessive repetition of “Tsar,” conveying Bunsha’s awkward Soviet-style social ritual in a royal setting. It’s not identical to the reference but achieves a similar effect. Slight loss: the reference’s “I’m the Tsar” self-introduction nuance (as if giving one’s name/title at a party) is softened; here it’s more like greeting the tsarina while addressing her as “Tsar,” which can momentarily confuse who is being labeled “Tsar.” Still, the scene context likely disambiguates.

items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Identical in meaning and function; preserves the sudden authoritarian, mock-royal performative stance. No issues.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me be, old woman, I’m grieving.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: Good overall: keeps the dismissive address “old woman” and the elevated melancholy. “Leave me be” is slightly more colloquial than the mock-archaic solemnity of the Russian, and “I’m grieving” is a bit more modern/psychological than “I am in sorrow,” but the contrast with the mundane setting remains and the line stays quotable.

items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: Mostly works: the repetition and attribution to “demons” is preserved, and the misunderstanding of technology as sorcery remains. However, dropping the object (“me”) makes the English slightly less clear/less personal (it can sound like a generic exclamation rather than “they trapped me”), and the lack of ellipsis reduces the melodramatic, lamenting cadence.

items.translation: That’s what the life-giving cross can do!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Accurate and functional: credits a religious miracle; keeps the “life-giving cross” phrase. Slightly less mock-biblical/ceremonial than “Behold the power…,” but still conveys the same comic conviction in a Soviet-modern context.

items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: Meaning is broadly conveyed (a call for everyone to dance), but the catchphrase-like, MC/announcer vibe of the Russian is flattened. “Everybody dance!” is grammatical but less idiomatic than “Everybody’s dancing!” / “Everyone dance!” / “All of you—dance!” and loses the sense of a formal proclamation opening the dance floor (which is part of the era-clash humor).

items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Captures the pragmatic function (self-satisfied delight at a fortuitous moment) and keeps it punchy. “Stop” is a bit spatially odd (he “dropped in” rather than made a “stop”), but it still reads as a breezy idiom and can work in subtitles. Slight loss of the “I dropped in well” burglar-charm nuance, but not damaging.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation:
Black caviar, red caviar…
Yes! Imported caviar…

Eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: The punchline “Eggplant!” lands, and the buildup list is preserved. But calling it “Imported caviar” is a creative/semantic misfire: the joke hinges on the Soviet product “икра баклажанная” (eggplant spread) being absurdly framed as “overseas caviar,” and in English it’s better not to label it “caviar” at all to avoid confusion (as the reference notes). Here, the viewer may think it’s literally some imported caviar variety, and then “Eggplant!” becomes less clearly a satirical downgrade. The cultural inversion (cheap spread treated as exotic delicacy) is weakened.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation:
Everything, everything earned
by backbreaking labor,
all gone: three tape recorders,
three movie cameras,

three domestic cigarette cases,
three suede jackets.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: Major accuracy problem: the source has a single “suede jacket” with a delayed “three” (he increments at the end), which is part of the comic effect (his list inflates; he fumbles the count). The translation turns it into “three suede jackets,” removing the incremental reveal and changing the joke mechanics. Also, “портсигара отечественных” is “domestic cigarette cases” (fine), but the rhythm of the list (staccato inventory with pauses) is smoothed into a more normal sentence, reducing the comedic bureaucratic rant feel.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation:
And you’ll be cured too!
And you’ll be cured too!

And I’ll be cured too!
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: Core gag is preserved: calm, rhythmic resignation that everyone will be “cured.” However, the agent is shifted: Russian implies an external “they” (the system/psych ward), which is important to the Soviet-psychiatry undertone; “you’ll be cured” is more generic and slightly less pointed. Also, the first clause in the sequence (“And they’ll cure you…”) is missing in this excerpted rendering (it starts at “too”), which weakens the escalating pattern if this is meant to cover the whole meme line.

summary.interpretation: 4.55 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.91 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
